result:
The air today has that specific density that makes every sound feel like it’s traveling through water—muffled but closer than it should be. I keep thinking about how cities are built on compacted time: every foundation stone is a fossil of someone’s yesterday, every steel beam a crystallized tomorrow when it was still imaginary. We walk around inside other people’s completed futures, complaining about rent.

I’ve been rereading old notebooks and realizing I used to write as if language were a net I could throw over the world and drag back to myself, heavy with unnameable creatures. Now it feels more like a handful of salt I’m tossing into the dark Pacific, just to watch the brief white constellation before it dissolves. That sounds defeatist; it isn’t. There’s a strange freedom in discovering you can’t own the ocean, only season a cup of it at a time.

Last night the neighbor’s cat slipped into my apartment, inspected every corner like a quality-control inspector, then left without comment. I appreciated the implied review: adequate shadows, edible plants, insufficient mystery. Animals are the last honest critics. They never pretend they’re not judging you.

I keep returning to a line from a deleted poem: “We grow up twice—once when we stop asking who put the moon on layaway, and once when we forgive them for the purchase.” I deleted it because it sounded clever, but I can’t evict it from my head. Maybe that’s the test: whatever refuses to leave the apartment of your skull is the rent you owe to yourself.

Sometimes I think the most radical act is simply to keep noticing: how the barista’s smile fractures slightly when she turns from you to the next customer, how the sirens here sound thinner than the ones in the town where you first understood the word emergency, how your own pulse accelerates when the plane tilts over the grid of lights that used to be merely metaphor. The world keeps handing out evidence that it isn’t finished, and we keep filing it under miscellaneous.

I’d like to write a story told entirely in marginalia: comments scrawled by one reader in a library book, answered years later by another, until the actual narrative becomes the negative space between their asynchronous conversation. A love affair conducted in defense of adverbs. A revolution plotted in complaints about the spine cracking. The book itself—some forgotten midlist novel about a lighthouse keeper—would stay mute, the perfect host.

But for now I sit at this small table, the cat-shaped absence still parked in the doorway, and watch the afternoon light settle on the chipped rim of a coffee cup. The moment feels paid for, like a parking ticket I’ve decided not to contest. Somewhere beneath the floorboards a neighbor practices the same trumpet phrase for the hundredth time, still flattening the third note. I root for him to get it right, knowing he won’t, knowing that’s not the point.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
